summary: McKenzie reports that his Mac version is performing surprisingly well, accounting for about 10% of his Windows user base despite minimal promotion. He discovers this through OS tracking he added to update requests. The Mac version isn't listed on major download sites yet, and the download process actively discourages Mac users by defaulting to the Windows installer. Despite these obstacles, Mac adoption significantly outpaces the 2% Mac share of his site visitors, prompting him to improve the Mac download experience with JavaScript-based OS detection.
key_points: Simple OS tracking in update requests revealed Mac users were 10% of Windows users despite only 2% of site visitors being on Macs
The Mac version was underperforming its potential due to a download flow that defaulted to Windows and required Mac users to find a secondary link
McKenzie's instinct to simplify by having one download button was actually costing him conversions from Mac users
Small instrumentation changes can reveal surprising user behavior that contradicts assumptions
money_quotes: That would put my Mac popularity at about 10% of my windows one, which is pretty phenomenal given that a) my Mac version isn't on the major download sites yet b) I make it almost painfully difficult to get the Mac version and c) 2% of my visitors use Macs.
I think I might have to revisit that whole "make getting the Mac version painfully difficult" thing.
I figure my users are not exactly savvy and having multiple download buttons confuses more users than the Mac downloads are worth.
While I still think that, inconveniencing 10% of my user base doesn't sit well with me, so I'm going to add a bit of Javascript to that download page and redirect Mac users to the correct package.
There is a note in bold telling Mac users "Sorry guys, that download is useless to you, click here".
key_insight: Measuring actual user behavior through simple instrumentation can reveal that design assumptions are actively hurting a significant segment of your customer base.
